title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Role of Philosophy in Modern Education

texts.A: In an increasingly technology-driven world, philosophy is too often viewed by policymakers, parents, and employers alike as a fossilized relic of a genuinely bygone and wholly irrelevant era. This is a truly tragic and costly misunderstanding of what the discipline actually teaches and develops. Philosophy is, in effect, the operating system of the human mind; it rigorously teaches us how to carefully analyze logical structure, systematically identify hidden fallacies, and construct genuinely coherent and robustly defensible arguments in complex situations. [38]Without these foundational critical skills, students are left entirely defenseless against the sophisticated and pervasive manipulation of modern digital advertising algorithms and the increasingly refined techniques of political propaganda.[/38] [40]We should therefore make critical inquiry a mandatory core subject from primary school onwards, meaningfully embedding it throughout the entire educational journey rather than confining it as an optional extra to university electives.[/40] [39]A society that can write brilliant code but fundamentally cannot think critically and ethically about what that code does and whom it harms is a society in profound and immediate peril.[/39]
texts.B: [37]The practical utility of a philosophy degree in the highly competitive current labor market is, if we are to be rigorously honest, undeniably and persistently low when assessed against standard economic and vocational metrics.[/37] While the broadly defined soft skills it cultivates are genuinely valuable in many professional and personal contexts, these capacities can typically be acquired more efficiently and at considerably lower financial cost through demanding and practically oriented subjects such as law, history, or rigorous advanced computer science programs with a critical thinking component. We should honestly stop encouraging ambitious young people to take on debilitating and potentially life-long financial debt for a qualification that currently offers no clear or reliably predictable career pathway in a competitive global marketplace. Education policy must ultimately align itself with the genuine economic needs of the nation and its future citizens, and in the present moment that unambiguously means prioritizing strategic STEM investment over abstract metaphysical speculation. Philosophy belongs in the personal library and thoughtful dinner table conversation, not in the taxpayer-funded classroom.
texts.C: I agree that the job-readiness argument levelled against philosophy as an academic discipline is a genuinely powerful and difficult proposition to fully dismiss, but it rests on the deeply questionable and rarely examined assumption that the only legitimate purpose of publicly funded education is to produce a compliant, immediately deployable, and economically productive workforce. That foundational assumption itself deserves serious and rigorous philosophical scrutiny. [38]I share the strongly held view that philosophy is absolutely essential for the functioning of a genuinely healthy and resilient democracy; it is arguably the only academic discipline that consistently and systematically asks why and ought we rather than merely how and what next.[/38] However, I firmly disagree with the narrow proposition that it should be taught as a standalone subject confined within its own departmental silo with no connection to other fields. [40]Philosophy is most effective and most transformatively powerful when it is genuinely and systematically integrated into other disciplines—rigorously examining the ethics of AI in computer science, or interrogating the moral and philosophical foundations of market economics in an economics lecture.[/40]
texts.D: The modern institutional obsession with narrowly defined and economically quantifiable STEM outcomes has quietly produced a notable crisis of existential meaning among young people who find themselves technically proficient but personally adrift and morally unequipped. Philosophy provides the essential and irreplaceable conceptual tools for individuals to consciously and deliberately construct their own coherent and robust ethical frameworks in an increasingly secular, pluralistic, and morally complex world. While I broadly share the legitimate skepticism surrounding the market value of a pure philosophy degree in its current institutional form, [37]I firmly believe that the core philosophical concepts of formal logic, careful ethical reasoning, and epistemic humility are considerably more genuinely practical and durable than many highly specific technical skills that demonstrably become professionally obsolete within a single decade of rapid technological change.[/37] [39]Learning to think clearly, rigorously, and consistently without personal prejudice or logical error is the most enduring and genuinely future-proof skill any educational system can cultivate.[/39] We should prioritize whole-person development over producing technically competent but unreflective workers.

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from B on the 'practicality' of philosophical skills?
questions.explanation: Expert B says philosophy has 'undeniably low' utility and no career path. Expert D argues that logic and ethics are actually 'more practical' and 'future-proof' than many technical skills.

questions.question: Which expert shares A's opinion about the necessity of philosophy for a healthy democracy?
questions.explanation: Expert A believes it protects against 'political propaganda.' Expert C agrees, stating that 'philosophy is essential for democracy.'

questions.question: Which expert expresses a similar view to D regarding the limitations of a purely technical education?
questions.explanation: Expert D says 'learning to think clearly' is better than technical skills that become 'obsolete.' Expert A says a society that can 'code but cannot think is a society in peril.'

questions.question: Which expert has a different view from C on whether philosophy should be taught as an independent subject?
questions.explanation: Expert C argues against it being a 'standalone subject' and prefers it integrated. Expert A explicitly states we should make it a 'mandatory subject' (as its own entity) from primary school onwards.
